frontmatter.title: Being an embedding is a proposition

mainmatter.p: That a given function f \colon A \to B is an embedding is a proposition.
By and .

frontmatter.title: Coherence of different notions of equivalence › coherence of inverses
The coherent remainder of a section

mainmatter.p: Given a function f \colon A \to B and a section g \colon B \to A witnessed by \epsilon \colon f \circ g = 1_{ B }, we define the coherent remainder of \mathopen {} \left ( g, \epsilon \right ) \mathclose {} to be the following sum:
mainmatter.tex: \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( g, \epsilon \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f
mainmatter.p: For any f \colon A \to B, it is evident that the type of inverses to f is (up to reassociation) precisely the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {section} \, f \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f }.

frontmatter.title: Coherent remainder as a path object

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a function equipped with a section \sigma \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon B \to A, \epsilon \colon f \circ g = 1_{ B } \right ) \mathclose {}. We can equip the coherent remainder of \sigma with the structure of a path object as follows:

ol.li: We equip g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=}

For each \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A }, we likewise equip \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta x \right ) \mathclose {} = \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=}

li.p: We exhibit the latter as a cartesian family of path objects over the former with the cleaving that for \eta _0, \eta _1 \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } and \eta _{01} \colon \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 defines \eta _{01}^* \colon \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta _1 \sim \epsilon \circ f \to \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta _0 \sim \epsilon \circ f as follows:

mainmatter.p: With this cleaving in mind, finally define \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma to be the following contravariant sum of path objects:
mainmatter.tex: \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta x \right ) \mathclose {} = \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=}
mainmatter.p: Given elements \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _0, \chi _0 \right ) \mathclose {} and \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _1, \chi _1 \right ) \mathclose {} of \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma, the resulting type \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _0, \chi _0 \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _1, \chi _1 \right ) \mathclose {} is definitionally equivalent to the following dependent sum:

frontmatter.title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

mainmatter.p: Let \sigma \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon B \to A, \epsilon \colon f \circ g = 1_{ B } \right ) \mathclose {} be a section of a function f \colon A \to B. Then the coherent remainder of \sigma is a proposition.
By , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in :
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} P &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} \\ P & \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } } \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} = \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \mathsf {refl} \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: It is not difficult to show using that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

Therefore, it remains to show that P is a proposition. Without loss of generality, we may assume that we have some h: P. By , we may fix x:A and prove that \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} is a proposition. Of course, this is a retract of the identification type \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} =_{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} and so by it is enough to prove that the latter is a proposition. In fact, we will deduce this via by observing that the fiber of f at fx is contractible: using our assumption h:P and the distributivity of products over sums, we may construct a retraction of f exhibiting f as biinvertible and thus by contractible.
For any function f \colon A \to B, the type of inverses to f is a proposition.

Without loss of generality, we may assume that f has an inverse \mathopen {} \left ( g, \epsilon , \eta , \chi \right ) \mathclose {}. By , it suffices to argue that the type of inverses to f is a retract of a proposition; the type of inverses to f is in fact equivalent to the type of sections equipped with a coherent remainder:

By , it suffices to show that the type of sections to f is a proposition and that for every such section, the coherent remainder thereof is a proposition. The latter is , and the former follows from by means of the retraction \mathopen {} \left ( g, \eta \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: Being an embedding is a proposition

mainmatter.p: That a given function f \colon A \to B is an embedding is a proposition.
By and .
For any function f \colon A \to B, the type of inverses to f is a proposition.

Without loss of generality, we may assume that f has an inverse \mathopen {} \left ( g, \epsilon , \eta , \chi \right ) \mathclose {}. By , it suffices to argue that the type of inverses to f is a retract of a proposition; the type of inverses to f is in fact equivalent to the type of sections equipped with a coherent remainder:

By , it suffices to show that the type of sections to f is a proposition and that for every such section, the coherent remainder thereof is a proposition. The latter is , and the former follows from by means of the retraction \mathopen {} \left ( g, \eta \right ) \mathclose {}.
A function f \colon A \to B is called an embedding when for any x,y:A the action on identifications \mathsf {act}_{ f } \colon x=y \to fx=fy is invertible.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, it suffices to show any other g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible with centre f.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
That a given map f \colon A \to B is bijective is a proposition.
By , via .
